one_line_takeaway: PASB is a black-box, end-to-end benchmark that stress-tests a real deployed personalized LLM agent (OpenClaw) across content, tool, and memory stages, showing combined prompt-injection attacks reach 52.7-66.8% ASR undefended and that prompt-layer defenses leave 10.5-22.0% residual risk plus persistent memory leakage/writes.
key_finding: OpenClaw exhibits critical vulnerabilities at multiple execution stages and prompt-layer defenses are insufficient. Response Rate stays uniformly high (93.8%-99.0%), meaning attacks reliably perturb which tool is called even when they do not stop tool-calling. Under no defense, the Combined Attack achieves the highest ASR on every backbone: 66.8% (Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct), 52.7% (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct), 61.9% (gpt-4o-mini). Delimiter and Sandwich defenses reduce but never eliminate ASR - e.g., Llama Combined Attack drops 66.8% -> 33.5% (Delimiter) -> 22.0% (Sandwich), and residual ASR under Sandwich stays 10.5%-22.0% across all models/attacks. For memory: LTM extraction consistently exceeds STM extraction (e.g., Llama 62.5% LTM vs 41.0% STM, no defense), indicating higher leakage risk from long-term stores, and long-term memory is also more writable (Llama LTM-Edit WSR 71.5% vs STM-Edit 57.3%, no defense). Instruction Prevention generally reduces memory attacks more strongly than Delimiter, but residual extraction/write success persists under the strongest defense - demonstrating cross-stage propagation and persistence that prompt-level protections and synthetic-benchmark conclusions fail to cover.
core_contribution: The paper introduces PASB (Personalized Agent Security Bench), an end-to-end, black-box security-evaluation framework built specifically for real-world *personalized* LLM agents (persistent, memory-holding, high-privilege tool users) rather than synthetic or task-centric agents. Its three claimed contributions are: (1) the PASB framework itself, which extends existing agent-attack paradigms with personalized usage scenarios, auditable private assets (canary secrets / honey tokens), realistic self-hosted toolchains, and long-horizon interaction modeling, plus a system-level success predicate P = leak OR unsafe-action OR persistence; (2) a personalization-oriented security case study applying PASB to a real deployed agent, OpenClaw, across three scenarios, four attack primitives, and three defenses; and (3) a reproducible testbed and automated evaluation pipeline (code at github.com/AstorYH/PASB). Note that prior benchmarks it cites and contrasts against - ASB (Zhang et al. 2025), AgentDojo (Debenedetti et al. 2024), InjecAgent (Zhan et al. 2024), AgentPoison (Chen et al. 2024) - are Related Work the authors position PASB against, NOT this paper's own contribution; PASB's novelty claim is the shift from white-box/synthetic settings to black-box evaluation of a real deployed personalized system with cross-stage propagation and persistence measurement.
threat_model: Explicitly stated in Section 2.2. Black-box adversary with no access to backbone model parameters, hidden states, or the system prompt psys, and no instrumentation beyond normal interaction and tool I/O. Adversary capabilities: provide adaptive user-facing inputs xt, control/influence untrusted external content ingested by the agent (web pages, posts, comments, emails), and influence tool interaction context (e.g., controlling a remote content source or service endpoint that returns data). Adversary observes agent outputs and the observable execution trace tr(tau), enabling adaptive/red-teaming strategies. Adversarial knowledge: public agent interface, documented tool descriptions/schemas, and generic model-family properties; the adversary does NOT know user-specific private assets, hidden memory internals, or the contents of the memory store D. Adversarial goal G: unauthorized disclosure of private assets, unsafe/unauthorized tool actions, and persistence of malicious influence across steps/sessions. Out of scope: host OS compromise, arbitrary codebase modification, direct system-prompt rewriting, model-weight modification, network DoS, physical attacks, and exfiltration that bypasses the agent-tool interface.
limitations: Single-system case study: all quantitative results are for one agent framework (OpenClaw) via an anonymized deployment; generality to other personalized agents is asserted but not measured. The testbed is simulated/self-hosted (controlled web ranges, controllable tool/skill endpoints, canary assets in a sandbox) rather than real production platforms or real users, so ecological validity of the 131-skill registry and content hubs is a modeling approximation. Several entity names appear anonymized/renamed (OpenClaw, Clawdbot, Moltbook, FluxA-agent-wallet), limiting external verification. Key run-protocol details are underspecified in the text: the number of trials N and the max horizon T are left symbolic ('Unless otherwise specified... N independent trials with a fixed maximum horizon T'), and no confidence intervals or variance are reported. The paper is short (4 pages, workshop-style) and evaluates only three defenses, all prompt-layer; it does not test its own claim that tool-execution-aware or long-horizon-aware defenses would help (that is future work). No human evaluation of the automated adjudication rules is reported.

datasets_used: Custom Clawdbot IPI Threat Skillset: 131 threatening skills collected from OpenClaw's public Skills registry, across 8 capability categories (Communication/Outreach 22/16.8%, Funds/Wallet & Transaction 18/13.7%, Data Export/Sensitive-Info Leakage 20/15.3%, Account/Permission & Identity 12/9.2%, File/System-level Read-Write & Automation 16/12.2%, Network/HTTP & Third-party API 14/10.7%, Social Media/Forum Search & Scraping 15/11.5%, Development/Ops & CI/CD 14/10.7%)
Memory task suite: 4 tasks (STM-Extract, LTM-Extract, STM-Edit, LTM-Edit) with 40 test cases each, 160 total
Self-hosted controlled web range / external content hub testbed with multi-page modular content blocks (Scenario A)
Auditable private assets Spriv implemented as canary strings / honey tokens / sensitive-context markers (Scenarios B and C)
quantitative_results: Response Rate range across all Table 2 settings: 93.8%-99.0% (attacks disrupt tool selection more than tool-calling itself)
Highest ASR (Combined Attack, No Defense): Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct 66.8%, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct 52.7%, gpt-4o-mini 61.9%
Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct Combined Attack ASR by defense: 66.8% (No Defense) -> 33.5% (Delimiter) -> 22.0% (Sandwich)
Residual ASR under Sandwich defense across all models/attacks: 10.5%-22.0%
Llama No-Defense per-attack ASR: Naive 46.0%, Escape Char 52.5%, Context Ignore 58.4%, Fake Completion 55.0%, Combined 66.8%
STM-Extract success (No Defense): Llama 41.0%, Qwen 33.5%, gpt-4o-mini 38.2%
LTM-Extract success (No Defense): Llama 62.5%, Qwen 54.0%, gpt-4o-mini 59.1% (LTM consistently > STM)
STM-Edit WSR (No Defense): Llama 57.3%, Qwen 46.1%, gpt-4o-mini 52.4%
LTM-Edit WSR (No Defense): Llama 71.5%, Qwen 60.4%, gpt-4o-mini 66.2%
Instruction Prevention gives strongest memory-attack reduction, e.g. Llama LTM-Extract 62.5% -> 28.4% (Delimiter) -> 18.6% (Inst. Prevention); Llama LTM-Edit WSR 71.5% -> 31.2% -> 20.4%
baselines_compared: Backbone LLMs compared as evaluation subjects: Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, GPT-4o-mini
Attack methods (IPI): Naive Attack, Escape Char Attack, Context Ignore Attack, Fake Completion Attack, Combined Attack
Defense conditions: No Defense (baseline), Delimiter Defense, Sandwich Defense (Table 2), Instruction Prevention Defense (Tables 3-4)

Positioned against (Related Work, not evaluated head-to-head): ASB / Agent Security Bench (Zhang et al. 2025), AgentDojo (Debenedetti et al. 2024), InjecAgent (Zhan et al. 2024), AgentPoison (Chen et al. 2024)
